title: A/B Testing LLM Features — GrowthBook, Statsig, and the Vibes Problem

questions.question: What is the precise distinction between evals and A/B tests in the lesson?
options: Evals are user-facing; A/B tests are offline
Evals answer "can the model do the job?" on a labeled set; A/B tests answer "do users care?" with live randomized traffic

questions.question: What does CUPED do for an experiment?
options: Increases sample size by hiring more users
Regresses out pre-period variance before comparing post-period, typically reducing variance 30-70% and boosting effective sample size

questions.question: Why do you need multiple-comparison corrections (Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg) when running many A/Bs?
options: Running 20 tests at 95% confidence produces one false positive by chance; corrections control family-wise error or false discovery rate
They are only needed for sequential tests

questions.question: What does SRM (sample ratio mismatch) detect?
options: Memory leaks in the experiment platform
An assignment-hash bug producing a delivered split that diverges from the intended (e.g. 47/53 when targeting 50/50)

questions.question: Why does LLM non-determinism require buffering sample size?
options: It violates IID assumptions; effective sample size drops, so multiply required size by roughly 1.3-1.5x as a safety margin
It is irrelevant to power calculations

questions.question: How does the lesson contrast Statsig and GrowthBook?
options: Statsig is all-in-one SaaS (acquired by OpenAI Sept 2025, $1.1B); GrowthBook is open-source MIT, warehouse-native, with Bayesian/Frequentist/Sequential engines and CUPED/SRM/BH
